text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
Post-Transplant Surveillance Study

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Female patient with history of acute myeloid leukemia, FLT3-ITD positive, treated with 7+3 induction chemotherapy (cytarabine/daunorubicin) plus midostaurin followed by myeloablative allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor, fludarabine/busulfan conditioning). This study is requested at approximately day +90 post-transplant for routine surveillance imaging to assess for residual or recurrent extramedullary leukemic disease, treatment response, and evaluation of post-transplant pulmonary findings.

COMPARISON:
Prior CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast performed pre-treatment (approximately 5 months prior) and whole-body PET-CT performed approximately 6 weeks post-diagnosis (pre-transplant restaging).

TECHNIQUE:
Axial images of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis were acquired using a standard helical CT protocol following intravenous administration of iohexol contrast. Sagittal and coronal multiplanar reformations were generated. Oral contrast was not administered.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways:
The central airways are patent. The trachea and main bronchi are unremarkable without narrowing or endobronchial lesion.

In the right lower lobe, there are two faint, hazy areas of ground-glass opacity, the larger measuring approximately 2.2 cm in greatest dimension situated in the posterior basal segment, and a smaller confluent focus in the lateral basal segment measuring approximately 1.0 cm. These areas are somewhat less dense and less extensive than would be expected for consolidative pneumonia and are not associated with air bronchograms or pleural-based density. The left lower lobe is clear. No new areas of consolidation are identified elsewhere in either lung. No discrete nodules, masses, cavities, or air-space opacification beyond the described ground-glass changes. The upper and middle lobes bilaterally are clear.

The bilateral pleural effusions noted on the pre-treatment study have completely resolved. No pleural thickening or pleural effusion is present.

Mediastinum and Hila:
The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The great vessels are unremarkable in caliber. The thoracic aorta is normal in size and contour without evidence of dissection or aneurysm. The pulmonary artery is normal in caliber.

The mediastinal contour is within normal limits. No mediastinal widening. No anterior, middle, or posterior mediastinal masses. No mediastinal lymph node enlargement. Multiple small mediastinal lymph nodes are present, all measuring well under 1 cm in short axis and without pathologic morphology. Bilateral hilar lymph nodes are not enlarged. No hilar masses.

Chest Wall, Pleura, and Diaphragm:
No chest wall soft tissue abnormality. No rib lesions. The diaphragms are intact. Incidentally noted: the visualized thyroid gland is unremarkable.

ABDOMEN:

Liver:
The liver demonstrates homogeneous enhancement following contrast administration. Liver size is normal, measuring approximately 15.8 cm in craniocaudal dimension on the right lobe (previously mildly enlarged at 17.1 cm on the pre-treatment study, subsequently normalized to 15.3 cm on pre-transplant PET-CT). No focal hepatic lesions detected. No biliary ductal dilatation. The intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. Gallbladder is present, without wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, or cholelithiasis.

Spleen:
The spleen is normal in size, measuring 10.5 cm in craniocaudal dimension. This represents a marked reduction from the presenting splenomegaly of 14.2 cm and is consistent with the pre-transplant PET-CT measurement of 11.8 cm, demonstrating continued normalization. The splenic parenchyma enhances homogeneously. No splenic lesions or accessory spleen noted.

Pancreas:
The pancreas is normal in size, morphology, and enhancement pattern. No focal pancreatic lesion. The main pancreatic duct is not dilated.

Kidneys and Adrenal Glands:
Both kidneys are normal in size and demonstrate symmetric enhancement with prompt contrast excretion. No hydronephrosis. No renal calculi. No focal renal lesion. The adrenal glands are normal bilaterally without nodule or mass.

Bowel:
The stomach is decompressed and unremarkable. Small bowel loops demonstrate normal caliber and wall thickness without evidence of obstruction, mucosal thickening, or mural stratification that would suggest engraftment syndrome or graft-versus-host disease. The colon is normal in caliber and wall thickness throughout. No free air. No evidence of bowel ischemia or perforation.

Vascular:
The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber. The celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, and inferior mesenteric artery are patent. The portal vein and hepatic veins are patent with normal hepatopetal flow pattern suggested on portal venous phase imaging. No filling defects to suggest thrombus in the major abdominal vasculature.

Lymph Nodes:
No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the abdomen. A small 6 mm peripancreatic lymph node and a 7 mm gastrohepatic ligament node are present, both within normal size limits and without pathologic morphology. No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. No para-aortic or paracaval nodal masses.

PELVIS:

Pelvic Organs:
The uterus is normal in size and morphology. No adnexal masses. No free fluid in the pelvis. The urinary bladder demonstrates normal wall thickness and is adequately distended. The pelvic musculature appears unremarkable.

Lymph Nodes:
No iliac, inguinal, or pelvic sidewall lymphadenopathy identified. No discrete soft tissue masses.

BONE WINDOWS:
The visualized osseous structures of the thorax, spine, pelvis, and proximal femora are carefully reviewed. No lytic or sclerotic lesions are identified. No cortical destruction. Vertebral body heights are maintained without compression fractures. Bone marrow signal, as assessed on the soft tissue windows, does not demonstrate focal regions of abnormal enhancement, fat replacement, or mass formation to suggest extramedullary disease within the marrow. No periosteal reaction.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Mild ground-glass opacification in the right lower lobe, with two faint foci in the posterior and lateral basal segments measuring up to 2.2 cm, present in a patient who is asymptomatic and approximately 90 days post-allogeneic stem cell transplant. The imaging appearance is nonspecific and could reflect a number of post-transplant processes including organizing pneumonia, resolving infectious bronchopneumonia, engraftment syndrome-related changes, or drug-related pulmonary toxicity. Active fungal or bacterial pneumonia is considered less likely given the absence of consolidation, nodularity, halo sign, or cavitation; however, close clinical correlation with symptoms, oxygen saturation, and pulmonary function is recommended. No new pulmonary parenchymal abnormalities beyond the described right lower lobe changes.

2. The previously documented splenomegaly has continued to normalize; the spleen now measures 10.5 cm, consistent with sustained treatment response and engraftment. Previously noted hepatomegaly has similarly resolved.

3. No lymphadenopathy identified in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis.

4. Complete resolution of the bilateral pleural effusions that were present on the pre-treatment study.

5. No focal hepatic, splenic, renal, or adrenal lesions. No ascites.

6. No osseous lesions to suggest extramedullary or cortical leukemic involvement.

7. No evidence of extramedullary AML recurrence on this examination.

Overall, the study is reassuring, with no imaging findings to suggest leukemic recurrence. The minor right lower lobe ground-glass changes are noted and warrant clinical follow-up; repeat imaging or bronchoscopy may be considered if the patient becomes symptomatic.

Signed: Attending Radiologist, Department of Radiology
